name: Earth Energy Grid
description: The Earth Energy Grid is a pseudoscientific theory proposing that the Earth is crisscrossed by a network of energy lines, sometimes referred to as ley lines, dragon lines, or Hartmann lines. Proponents suggest that these lines are natural pathways for electromagnetic or subtle energy, and that ancient sacred sites, megalithic structures, and other significant locations were deliberately built along these lines to harness or interact with this energy. The theory often incorporates concepts from geomancy, dowsing, and spiritual beliefs. Key evidence cited includes the apparent alignment of certain historical sites on straight lines, the reported experiences of dowsers feeling energy flows, and anecdotal accounts of paranormal activity at grid points. Mainstream science dismisses the Earth Energy Grid as lacking empirical evidence and scientific validity. The perceived alignments are often attributed to chance or confirmation bias, and the claimed energy flows are not detectable by conventional scientific instruments. Critics argue that the theory relies on subjective interpretation and lacks testable predictions. The concept has gained traction within New Age and esoteric circles, often associated with ideas of planetary consciousness and spiritual awakening.
subtitle: Global network of subtle energy pathways

claims.claim: Ancient sites are aligned on ley lines.
claims.evidence: Apparent linear arrangements of sites like Stonehenge, Giza pyramids, and Machu Picchu.
claims.criticism: Statistical analysis shows alignments are no more frequent than expected by random chance. Confirmation bias in selecting sites.

claims.claim: Ley lines carry a measurable energy.
claims.evidence: Dowsers claim to detect energy flows using rods or pendulums.
claims.criticism: Dowsing lacks scientific validation; results are inconsistent and attributable to the ideomotor effect or unconscious cues.

claims.claim: Grid points are locations of paranormal activity.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports of heightened psychic experiences, UFO sightings, and strange phenomena at specific locations.
claims.criticism: Anecdotal evidence is unreliable and subject to misinterpretation. Lack of controlled scientific studies to confirm paranormal activity.

keyFigures.name: Alfred Watkins
keyFigures.role: Popularized the concept of ley lines in 'The Old Straight Track'

keyFigures.name: Paul Devereux
keyFigures.role: Researcher of earth mysteries and ancient alignments

keyFigures.name: David Rohl
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist critical of hyperdiffusionist claims about global alignments

timeline.event: Alfred Watkins publishes 'The Old Straight Track', popularizing ley lines in England.
Ley line research expands with the rise of New Age spirituality and earth mysteries movements.
Continued interest in Earth Energy Grids through books, websites, and alternative media.

sources.author: Devereux, Paul
sources.work: Earth Memory: Practical Examples Using Sacred Geometry
sources.title: Devereux, Paul

sources.author: Rohl, David
sources.work: Test of Time: The Bible - from Myth to History
sources.title: Rohl, David
